title: M1a — Durable payment/order handshake (prove the loop)
description:
## Outcome
A single-box lnrent daemon that proves the full rent loop end to end with a trivial, instant recipe: publish a NIP-99 listing, take a NIP-17 order, reserve capacity, issue a Fedimint invoice, capture the settlement idempotently, provision, deliver credentials over a NIP-17 DM, let the buyer manage the rented service through one ops interface, and run the prepaid-expiry subscription lifecycle (renew / suspend / destroy) with capture-then-refund and crash recovery.

## Why this exists
The order/payment handshake is the product and its riskiest part. M1a de-risks it independent of VM/Incus/Iroh complexity (M1b/M1c) by exercising the whole durable money path against a trivial recipe (a WireGuard peer or a dummy service). If this is correct, everything else is recipes.

## Scope
The handshake control plane only: store actor, the Fedimint backend payment client, Nostr engine (NIP-99 + NIP-17 DM protocol), recipe runner + one trivial recipe, pre-flight + capacity reservation, idempotent capture, reconcile-loop state machine + downtime credit, provision + delivery outbox, capture-then-refund ledger, the CLI<->daemon IPC surface, the buyer service-management ops dispatch (recipe-declared operations dispatched through one buyer interface, request-kind in M1a — §7.4/ADR-0013), a minimal CLI buyer and a web WASM buyer (browser access), operator identity/config bootstrap, and the end-to-end money-path tests.

## Non-goals
No Incus/VM provisioning, no Iroh/Tor reachability (so no interactive-kind ops), no security tiers beyond Tier 0 labeling, no per-box key split / operator manifest (single account-0 key), no NWC pull. Those are M1b/M1c/M5. (The web WASM buyer IS in M1a — see Scope.)

## Critical constraints
AI-free control plane (pure Rust, no LLM at runtime — including every management op). Daemon is the SOLE writer of sqlite (ADR-0001). The Fedimint backend/Fedimint cannot hold invoices, so capture-then-refund (ADR-0003). Durable, crash-safe handshake (ADR-0009). Management ops are a recipe's declared narrow surface, never arbitrary host shell (ADR-0013).

## Spec refs
SPEC.md v0.26 §5.1, §6 (§6.2/§6.3/§6.5/§6.6), §7.4, §9.3, §11; ADR-0001, ADR-0003, ADR-0005, ADR-0009, ADR-0013; eng-review T1-T14.

## Success Criteria
- The full M1a loop runs on one box with the trivial recipe: list -> order -> reserve -> invoice -> pay -> capture -> provision -> deliver -> renew -> suspend -> destroy.
- A buyer can run a recipe-declared management operation (e.g. status/restart) on an active subscription through the same buyer interface; an unauthorized op is refused.
- All four mandatory money-path tests pass green in CI (see lnrent-7fp.15).
- The daemon can be killed at any handshake step and recovers correctly on restart.
- A buyer who pays always ends in exactly one of: credentials delivered, or funds refunded. Never stranded, never silently charged.
- The web WASM buyer (lnrent-7fp.18) completes the full loop in a headless browser (browser marketplace access), after the CLI money-path gate (lnrent-7fp.15).

title: Store actor + sqlite layer (sole writer)
description:
## Outcome
One tokio task owns the rusqlite Connection and is the only writer; reconcile, Nostr, payment, and IPC tasks send typed commands over an mpsc channel and await replies. Provides transactional CRUD for all §11 tables and the atomic multi-row transitions the handshake needs (e.g. invoice OPEN->PAID + sub PENDING->PROVISIONING in one txn).

## Why
ADR-0001: the daemon is the sole writer so the AI-free invariant is structural, not a convention, and there are no write races. The actor keeps rusqlite (sync) on one thread.

## Scope
store module, command enum + mpsc, transaction helpers, event_log append on every transition, sqlite WAL mode, the §11 schema (already in store.rs: **10 tables** — operator, recipe, subscription, invoice, event_log, reservation, daemon_state, refund_attempt, outbox, op_invocation). `op_invocation` (PRIMARY KEY (sender_pubkey, request_id)) is the durable idempotency record for buyer management ops (§7.4, ADR-0013): the operator op-dispatch (lnrent-7fp.20) inserts before running a hook and caches the result/error for duplicate resend.

## Non-goals
Business logic (capture/reconcile/refund/op-dispatch call the store; they live in their own beads).

## Constraints / failure handling
Sole writer; every mutation journaled to event_log; atomic captures use one transaction; WAL so reads never block the writer; no lock held across an await. The `op_invocation` unique key makes a duplicate op.request a no-op insert (the dispatcher then resends the cached result rather than re-running a non-idempotent hook).

## Dependencies
Depends on: injectable clock (lnrent-7fp.2). Unblocks: migrations, reservation, capture, reconcile, provision, refund, IPC, backup, operator-init, op-dispatch (lnrent-7fp.20).

## Verification
Unit: each txn helper + the atomic capture txn (rollback on error). Keep the schema test (**10 tables**). A test that concurrent command senders serialize. A test that a second insert with the same (sender_pubkey, request_id) is rejected/no-ops (op idempotency).

## Spec refs
ADR-0001, ADR-0013; SPEC §11; eng-review T1.

## Acceptance Criteria
- All 10 §11 tables exist; the schema test passes.
- The capture transaction (invoice OPEN->PAID + sub PENDING->PROVISIONING + event_log append) commits or rolls back atomically (unit test proves rollback on injected error).
- Every write goes through the actor; a concurrency test shows commands serialize with no lost update.
- WAL is enabled; a read does not block a concurrent write.
- A duplicate `op_invocation` insert on the same (sender_pubkey, request_id) does not create a second row (idempotency key for lnrent-7fp.20).

title: Provision + delivery outbox (crash-safe)
description:
## Outcome
The crash-safe settle->capture->provision->deliver sequence. On PROVISIONING, run the recipe provision hook (idempotent, re-run on restart); on success, in ONE transaction move the subscription -> ACTIVE, set paid_through = now + period, and write an outbox row carrying the provision.ready DM. A sender task drains the outbox, publishes the NIP-17 DM, marks it SENT, and retries until sent. A crash after ACTIVE but before send re-sends on restart, so a paid buyer is never stranded.
## Why
If ACTIVE is committed before the credentials DM is delivered, the buyer paid but may never receive access; relays also drop DMs. The durable outbox guarantees delivery and is the dropped-DM resync answer (codex #6 / ADR-0009).
## Scope
provision invocation via the recipe runner; the atomic ACTIVE+outbox transaction; the outbox sender (drain/publish/retry/mark SENT); restart re-drive of PROVISIONING and unsent outbox.
## Non-goals
Refund on provision failure (refund bead); the Nostr publish primitive (nostr bead).
## Constraints / failure handling
provision hooks idempotent; outbox write is in the same txn as ACTIVE; sender retries with backoff; restart re-runs stuck PROVISIONING and resends unsent outbox rows.
## Dependencies
Depends on: store, recipe runner, nostr, capture. Unblocks: e2e.
## Verification
Integration: kill the daemon between ACTIVE and outbox-send -> the DM is resent on restart; the buyer processes provision.ready idempotently (at-least-once delivery, deduped by message id).
## Spec refs
SPEC §5.1, §6.6; ADR-0009; eng-review T4.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Kill the daemon between sub->ACTIVE and outbox-send: provision.ready is resent on restart (integration).
- provision.ready is delivered at-least-once and deduped by a stable message id (NOT exactly-once network delivery); the buyer processes it idempotently.
- A subscription stuck in PROVISIONING is re-driven on restart.

## Note (codex)
The ACTIVE transaction also CONSUMES the order's reservation (HELD->CONSUMED, lnrent-7fp.7).

title: Capture-then-refund + refund ledger
description:
## Outcome
Capture-then-refund with a durable ledger. On permanent provision failure (after retries) -> REFUND_DUE + a refund_attempt row (dest from order.request, amount, status PENDING). A refunder pays via PaymentBackend::pay (records backend_payment_id), checks status via payment_status, retries; after N failures the subscription stays REFUND_DUE and the operator is alerted (billing.refund DM status=failed). Also handles settled-but-expired (a settlement after invoice expiry -> auto-refund, order not resurrected).
## Why
phoenixd/Fedimint can't hold invoices, so funds are captured before provisioning; if provision fails (or a payment lands on an expired order) the buyer must be made whole without a manual op (ADR-0003). BOLT12-offer/LN-address refunds can fail to an offline payer, so the ledger must retry and never double-pay.
## Scope
the refund_attempt ledger lifecycle; the refunder task (pay/status/retry/alert); executing settled-but-expired refunds (detection/creation owned by lnrent-7fp.8); the billing.refund DM.
## Non-goals
Receiving (the payment backend bead); the capture transition (capture bead).
## Constraints / failure handling
never double-pay (dedupe on backend_payment_id); REFUND_DUE-stuck after N attempts alerts the operator; refund dest collected up front in order.request (BOLT12 offer preferred, LN address fallback).
## Dependencies
Depends on: store, phoenixd, reconcile. Unblocks: e2e.
## Verification
Integration: provision failure -> auto-refund to a test dest; refund send fails -> stays REFUND_DUE + alert, no double-pay on retry; settled-but-expired -> refund not provision.
## Spec refs
SPEC §6.3, §6.4, §6.6; ADR-0003, ADR-0009; eng-review T7; codex.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Provision failure -> auto-refund to a test dest; subscription -> REFUNDED (integration).
- Refund send fails -> subscription stays REFUND_DUE + operator alert; a retry does NOT double-pay (dedupe on backend_payment_id).
- Settled-but-expired -> refund_attempt, not provision.

## Note (codex)
Releases the reservation on REFUND_DUE (lnrent-7fp.7). Sends billing.refund (status sent/failed) to the buyer by enqueueing a billing.refund outbox row (delivered by the sender in lnrent-7fp.10).

title: lnrent CLI <-> daemon IPC surface
description:
## Outcome
A local CLI<->daemon IPC surface: a Unix-domain socket the daemon owns, with a small JSON/line command set (status, recipes, subs, and admin: force suspend/resume, inspect a subscription). The lnrent CLI and the Claude skills act ONLY through this; they never touch sqlite directly.
## Why
ADR-0001 says skills act through the lnrent CLI with the daemon as sole writer; the surface was never specified. Without it skills hit sqlite directly and break the invariant (eng-review #2).
## Scope
the UDS server in the daemon (commands routed to the store actor); the lnrent CLI client wired to it (replace the M0 stub prints); read commands + a few admin mutations.
## Non-goals
Remote/networked admin API (forbidden by the VM guidelines); the buyer client (separate bead).
## Constraints / failure handling
socket in the data dir with tight perms; every mutation goes through the store actor (sole writer) and is journaled; no public/networked exposure.
## Dependencies
Depends on: store. Unblocks: e2e (operator-side checks).
## Verification
Unit/integration: lnrent status/recipes/subs return live state via the socket; an admin suspend goes through the store actor and is journaled.
## Spec refs
ADR-0001; SPEC §4.2, §10; eng-review architecture #2.

## Acceptance Criteria
- lnrent status / recipes / subs return live state via the Unix socket (integration).
- An admin suspend routes through the store actor and is journaled to event_log.
- The socket is not network-reachable.

title: Minimal CLI buyer
description:
## Outcome
A shared **buyer-core** Rust lib (the lnrent DM protocol, order flow, gift-wrap encode/decode, AND the unified service-management `ops` interface — §7.4, with injectable relay/signer/payer seams) that is target-agnostic (compiles native AND wasm32), plus a thin native **CLI buyer** over it: discover a NIP-99 listing, send order.request (+ refund dest + params), receive order.invoice, show the bolt11, receive provision.ready, print credentials — and after delivery, manage the rented service through the same client.

## Why
M1a needs a buyer side to prove list->order->pay->provision->deliver. Extracting buyer-core (instead of a CLI-only impl) lets the web WASM buyer (lnrent-7fp.18) reuse ONE protocol implementation. The same buyer-core is where a rented service is managed (ADR-0013): one client both rents AND operates, with no per-service tooling.

## Scope
buyer-core lib (protocol/order/gift-wrap; relay, signer, payer as injected traits); the unified `ops` surface — discover a subscription's recipe-declared operations (from the Listing's published recipe metadata), build/send op.request, await op.result for `request`-kind ops; the native CLI shell (local-key signer, pay-by-copy bolt11, native WS relays) with `lnrent-buyer ops <sub> list` / `lnrent-buyer ops <sub> <op> [params]`.

## Non-goals
The WASM/browser shell (lnrent-7fp.18); operator manifest verification (M5); embedded wallet. `interactive`-kind ops (shell/console/logs -f over the Iroh Native-connect session, §9.2) — the ops interface RECOGNIZES the kind and routes to a (deferred, M1b+) Iroh transport seam, it does not silently drop it; only `request`-kind ops are wired in M1a.

## Constraints / failure handling
buyer-core must avoid native-only deps in its core path so it compiles to wasm32; signer/relay/payer (and the deferred Iroh interactive transport) are injected per target.

## Dependencies
Depends on: the wire codec (lnrent-7fp.19, incl. op.request/op.result). Injects its own relay/signer/payer seams; it does NOT depend on the operator engine (lnrent-7fp.5). The ops-interface integration runs against the operator-side dispatch (lnrent-7fp.20) and is gated jointly in the e2e (lnrent-7fp.15), so this bead's own tests need only the codec. Unblocks: e2e (lnrent-7fp.15), web WASM buyer (lnrent-7fp.18).

## Verification
Integration against a running daemon + local relay + payment-backend mock for the order flow; buyer-core unit-tests the op.request/op.result encode/decode path through the codec. Full ops integration (real op.result from the operator) is proven in lnrent-7fp.15.

## Spec refs
SPEC §3, §4.2, §5.1, §7.4, §14; ADR-0013; eng-review T13.

## Acceptance Criteria
- buyer-core compiles for BOTH native and wasm32 (no native-only deps in the core path).
- The CLI buyer completes a full order -> pay -> receive-creds against a running daemon + local relay + payment-backend mock.
- The signer/relay/payer seams are injectable so the web shell can supply NIP-07 / browser-WS / WebLN.
- buyer-core exposes an `ops` interface that lists a subscription's declared operations and dispatches a `request`-kind op (op.request -> op.result); the CLI surfaces `ops <sub> list` and `ops <sub> <op>`.
- An `interactive`-kind op is recognized and routed to the deferred Iroh transport seam, not silently dropped.

title: Daemon state backup
description:
## Outcome
A backup mechanism for the daemon's own sqlite state (subscriptions, invoices, paid_through, reservations, refund ledger, outbox) so the operator's commitments survive box death even though the BIP39 seed is backed up separately.
## Why
ADR-0004 forces seed backup, but losing the sqlite loses who-paid-through-when for a system holding other people's prepaid time (eng-review #5). 
## Scope
a periodic consistent sqlite backup (online backup API or litestream-style), operator-configurable destination; a documented restore path.
## Non-goals
Multi-box replication/HA (M7); seed backup (ADR-0004).
## Dependencies
Depends on: store. P2 (not a ship blocker for the loop, but required before real money).
## Verification
Integration: kill+restore from backup reproduces subscription/paid_through/ledger state.
## Spec refs
eng-review (inferred requirement; SPEC §13 is security, not backup); eng-review T14; codex.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Kill + restore from backup reproduces subscription / paid_through / reservation / refund-ledger state (integration).

title: M1a end-to-end loop + money-path tests (ship gate)
description:
## Outcome
The M1a ship gate: an end-to-end test that runs the whole loop on one box against a payment-backend mock (Fedimint test federation) and a local relay with the trivial recipe — publish listing -> order.request -> pre-flight+reserve -> invoice -> pay -> idempotent capture -> provision -> provision.ready delivered -> run a buyer management op -> renew -> suspend -> destroy. PLUS the mandatory money-path tests (these gate ship): idempotent-capture replay (no double-extend), settled-but-expired auto-refund (no silent theft), crash recovery (kill between capture and deliver -> recovered), and downtime credit (operator outage doesn't suspend a buyer unfairly).
## Why
M1a's whole purpose is to prove the handshake is correct. These are money paths: if any is wrong and silent, funds vanish or buyers are stranded. They are non-negotiable (eng-review failure-modes; outside-voice; codex). M1a also proves the buyer can MANAGE what they rented through the unified ops interface (ADR-0013), so a minimal request-kind op is part of the gate.
## Scope
the e2e harness (daemon + local relay + payment-backend mock + trivial recipe + CLI buyer); the four mandatory money-path tests; the minimal buyer-op proof (a request-kind op through op.request/op.result + a refused unauthorized op); CI wiring.
## Non-goals
VM/Incus, Iroh/Tor, tiers (M1b/M1c); interactive/Iroh ops (M1b+).
## Constraints
deterministic via the injectable clock; no real funds.
## Dependencies
Depends on: reservation, capture, reconcile, provision+outbox, refund, IPC, buyer, AND the operator-side op dispatch (lnrent-7fp.20) for the buyer-op proof. This is the milestone-completion bead.
## Verification
This bead IS the verification. Acceptance signal: the full loop test + all four money-path tests + the buyer-op proof pass green in CI.
## Spec refs
SPEC §6.6, §7.4, §15 (M1a); ADR-0003/0005/0009/0013; eng-review failure modes + Implementation Tasks.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Full loop test green: list -> order -> reserve -> invoice -> pay -> capture -> provision -> deliver -> renew -> suspend -> destroy.
- Money-path 1 (idempotent capture): a duplicate settlement extends paid_through exactly once.
- Money-path 2 (settled-but-expired): a late settlement auto-refunds, no provision, no kept funds.
- Money-path 3 (crash recovery): killing the daemon after capture, mid-provision, and after ACTIVE-before-deliver each recovers correctly.
- Money-path 4 (downtime credit): an operator outage across a renewal window does NOT suspend the buyer on restart.
- Ops path: after provision.ready, the buyer runs a request-kind op (status/restart) via the CLI and receives a matching `op.result` (request_id correlated); a DUPLICATE op.request (same id) resends the cached result and the non-idempotent `restart` hook runs exactly once; an op.request from a non-buyer key is refused (`unauthorized`, no hook run, no subscription-existence leak).
- All deterministic via TestClock, no real funds, wired into CI.

## Scope note (codex)
This is the CLI/daemon money-path gate (the loop proven via the CLI buyer). The web WASM buyer (lnrent-7fp.18) is the final browser-access proof and runs AFTER this gate; M1a is complete only when BOTH lnrent-7fp.15 and lnrent-7fp.18 are green.

title: Operator identity + config bootstrap
description:
## Outcome
Bootstrap the operator: from a BIP39 seed derive the account-0 Nostr identity (NIP-06, m/44'/1237'/0'/0/0), persist the operator row (master_pubkey, op_pubkey, box_index=0), and load runtime config — the Fedimint federation invite + gateway, and the default relay set. Enough for the daemon to sign listings/DMs and receive payments in M1a.

## Why
The handshake cannot run without an operator identity + backend/relay config. M1a uses a single account-0 key (the master/per-box split + operator manifest are M5, ADR-0004). Without this nothing can sign a 30402 or open the Fedimint client / gateway connection.

## Scope
seed -> account-0 key (NIP-06) via rust-nostr; operator-row persistence through the store actor; load relays + the Fedimint federation invite + gateway from config / data dir.

## Non-goals
Per-box operational keys + operator manifest (M5); the lnrent-onboard skill UX; seed backup (ADR-0004).

## Constraints / failure handling
single key in M1a (account 0 = master, used directly); seed/key live in the data dir with tight perms, never logged.

## Dependencies
Depends on: store (lnrent-7fp.1). Used by the Nostr engine + payment config at wire-up. Unblocks: e2e.

## Verification
Unit: deterministic derivation from a fixed seed.

## Spec refs
ADR-0004, SPEC §4.6, §11 (operator table).

## Acceptance Criteria
- From a fixed test seed, the account-0 npub is deterministic and matches a NIP-06 derivation vector.
- The operator row is persisted (master_pubkey, op_pubkey, box_index=0) via the store actor.
- The daemon signs and publishes a 30402 listing with the derived key.
- the Fedimint federation invite + gateway, and default relays, load from config.

## Seed derivation for the payment backend (codex)
- The Fedimint client root secret derives from the operator seed at a DEDICATED path, distinct from the NIP-06 Nostr account paths; deterministic from a fixed seed (test vector), so one seed backs up identity AND ecash (ADR-0004/0012, §4.6).

title: Order intake + invoice issuance
description:
## Outcome
The order-intake + invoice-issuance integrator that owns ADR-0009's "the PENDING subscription IS the order." Two paths:
- New order: on order.request, validate params + pre-flight/reserve capacity (lnrent-7fp.7), create the subscription PENDING + an invoice OPEN with a unique external_id in ONE store transaction, call the Fedimint backend create_invoice(external_id), and send order.invoice (or order.error + release the reservation on validation/capacity failure).
- Renewal invoice: at soft_date (driven by reconcile, lnrent-7fp.9) or on renew.request, issue a renewal invoice (new invoice row + create_invoice) and send billing.invoice.

## Why
Launch review: no bead owned the order->invoice orchestration; capture/reservation/payment/nostr each held a piece but "create the PENDING sub + OPEN invoice and send order.invoice" was orphaned. ADR-0009 makes the PENDING sub the durable order that settlement correlates to via external_id.

## Scope
order.request handling; the create-PENDING-sub + OPEN-invoice transaction; the Fedimint backend create_invoice with external_id; sending order.invoice/order.error/billing.invoice via the Nostr transport (lnrent-7fp.5); renewal-invoice issuance.

## Non-goals
Settlement capture (lnrent-7fp.8); provisioning (lnrent-7fp.10); the DM transport primitives (lnrent-7fp.5); deadline firing (lnrent-7fp.9).

## Constraints / failure handling
PENDING sub + OPEN invoice created in one transaction so the order is durable before the invoice is shown; on failure send order.error and release the reservation, leaving no dangling PENDING sub; external_id unique per invoice; duplicate order.request (same request id) is idempotent.

## Dependencies
Depends on: store (lnrent-7fp.1), the Fedimint backend (lnrent-7fp.4), Nostr transport (lnrent-7fp.5), reservation (lnrent-7fp.7). Unblocks: reconcile renewal (lnrent-7fp.9), e2e (lnrent-7fp.15).

## Verification
Integration over a local relay + payment-backend mock.

## Spec refs
SPEC §5.1, §6.2, §6.4, §6.6; ADR-0009.

## Acceptance Criteria
- order.request -> a PENDING subscription + an OPEN invoice (unique external_id) are created in one transaction and order.invoice is sent with the bolt11.
- Invalid params or insufficient capacity -> order.error sent + reservation released; no dangling PENDING sub.
- At soft_date or on renew.request, a renewal invoice is issued and billing.invoice is sent.
- A duplicate order.request (same request id) does not create a second order; it resends the existing order.invoice (or current order status) — this is also how a dropped order.invoice is recovered (lnrent-7fp.5).

## Ordering / atomicity (codex)
Reserve capacity (lnrent-7fp.7), then create the PENDING sub + OPEN invoice in ONE store transaction, then call the Fedimint backend create_invoice. If create_invoice or persistence fails, RELEASE the reservation and fail the order, leaving no dangling PENDING sub or stuck reservation.

title: Web WASM buyer (browser marketplace access)
description:
## Outcome
A static **web (WASM) buyer**: buyer-core (lnrent-7fp.13) compiled to wasm32 inside a minimal browser SPA so the marketplace is usable from a website with no backend. Browse NIP-99 listings, place an order, pay the Lightning invoice, receive credentials — and run the browser-feasible subset of the service-management `ops` interface — all in the browser.

## Why
The two-sided marketplace needs a website, not just a CLI. A WASM client reuses the one Rust protocol implementation (no duplicate JS protocol), and static hosting preserves the no-central-server property (§1): the site is a pure buyer front-end over relays + the buyer's wallet. The same site should also let a buyer manage what they rented (ADR-0013), at least for request/response ops.

## Scope
wasm32 build of buyer-core (wasm-pack/trunk); a minimal SPA (browse listings, order form, invoice/pay, creds view, and a service-management view); browser glue — NIP-07 (window.nostr) signer, WebLN (window.webln) payer with copy-bolt11/QR fallback, relays over browser WebSockets; the `request`-kind ops subset (op.request/op.result over the same buyer-core); static hosting.

## Non-goals
Operator/seller UI (operator side stays daemon/CLI/skills); a hosted backend (must stay static); embedded custodial wallet; the VM access/reachability plane (separate from buying); `interactive`-kind ops (Iroh shell/console/logs -f, §9.2) — out of browser scope for M1a (the buyer-core seam exists but the web shell does not wire an Iroh transport).

## Critical constraints / risks
FIRST STEP is a feasibility spike: rust-nostr must build + run NIP-17/44/59 gift-wrap on wasm32 (the key gate). NIP-07 / WebLN need a compatible extension/wallet -> ship the copy-bolt11/QR fallback. No secrets in the static bundle.

## Dependencies
Depends on: buyer-core + CLI buyer (lnrent-7fp.13) and a working loop to test against (lnrent-7fp.15, which also gates the operator-side op dispatch). Follows the CLI buyer.

## Verification
A headless-browser e2e drives the SPA through the full loop, including a request-kind management op.

## Spec refs
SPEC §1, §3, §5.1, §7.4, §9.2, §14; ADR-0013; eng-review (web client, formerly M4).

## Acceptance Criteria
- The SPA loads as static files (no backend) and connects to relays over browser WebSockets.
- A headless-browser test completes the full buyer loop: browse a listing -> order.request -> pay the invoice (WebLN, mockable) -> receive provision.ready -> display credentials.
- Signing uses NIP-07 (embedded-key fallback, export-prompted since it decrypts creds); paying uses WebLN (copy-bolt11/QR fallback).
- A buyer with NEITHER NIP-07 nor WebLN completes the loop via embedded key + copy-bolt11/QR (the headless-browser test covers this fallback path).
- The SPA runs a `request`-kind management op (e.g. status) on an active subscription and displays op.result.data.
- buyer-core is shared with the CLI (lnrent-7fp.13) -- no duplicated protocol logic.

## Capability degradation (grill)
Detect and degrade: signing prefers NIP-07, else an embedded key the SPA generates + persists (and prompts to export/back up, since it decrypts credentials); paying prefers WebLN, else copy-bolt11/QR. A buyer with no Nostr extension and no WebLN wallet — just a phone Lightning wallet — can still complete the loop.

title: lnrent wire-codec lib (DM types + gift-wrap + 30402)
description:
## Outcome
A shared, target-agnostic Rust lib for the lnrent wire protocol: the DM message types (order.request, order.invoice, order.error, provision.ready, billing.invoice, billing.notice, billing.refund, renew.request, sub.cancel, op.request, op.result) as typed structs with JSON ser/de; NIP-17 gift-wrap (NIP-59/44) encode/decode of those messages; and the NIP-99 (30402) listing build/parse, INCLUDING the recipe's published operation declarations (name/label/kind/params — NOT `hook`) so a buyer can discover the ops surface from a listing (§5.4, §7.4). Pure codec, no I/O. Compiles native AND wasm32. This bead PINS the on-wire schema (§5.4 listing layout + DM payloads, incl. op.request `id` and op.result `request_id`).

## Why
Both the OPERATOR Nostr engine (lnrent-7fp.5) and the BUYER buyer-core (lnrent-7fp.13) must speak the exact same wire format — including the service-management op.request/op.result pair (§7.4, ADR-0013). Owning it once stops operator/buyer drift and removes the wrong coupling of buyer-core depending on the operator's daemon engine. wasm32 support lets the web buyer (lnrent-7fp.18) reuse it.

## Scope
message-type structs + JSON ser/de (incl. op.request/op.result); gift-wrap encode/decode (rust-nostr NIP-59/44); 30402 listing build/parse (incl. the recipe's `[[operation]]` declarations — name/label/kind/params); stable per-message id for idempotent dedupe. Pure functions — no relay/network/signer/payment I/O (those are seams in the consumers).

## Non-goals
Relay connections, signing, payment (consumers inject those); operator manifest (M5). The op DISPATCH logic (auth, running hooks) is the operator-side bead lnrent-7fp.20 and the buyer-side bead lnrent-7fp.13 — this bead is only the typed op.request/op.result codec they share.

## Constraints / risks
Must compile to wasm32 — this is the EARLIEST place to run the rust-nostr NIP-44/59 gift-wrap-on-wasm feasibility check that also gates the web buyer (lnrent-7fp.18).

## Dependencies
None (root). Unblocks: operator Nostr engine (lnrent-7fp.5), buyer-core (lnrent-7fp.13), operator op dispatch (lnrent-7fp.20).

## Verification
Unit: encode->decode round-trips identically for every message type (including op.request/op.result), including through NIP-17 gift-wrap; a 30402 listing builds and parses; the crate builds for native AND wasm32.

## Spec refs
SPEC §5.1, §5.3, §7.4; ADR-0009, ADR-0013.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Every DM message type round-trips through JSON and through NIP-17 gift-wrap (encode->decode == identity), including op.request (with `id`) and op.result (with `request_id` + structured `error {code,message,retryable}`).
- A NIP-99 30402 listing builds and parses, and round-trips the recipe's published operation declarations (name/label/kind/params; `hook` is never serialized into a listing) — build->parse == identity, with a schema `version` field and unknown-field tolerance.
- The crate compiles for native AND wasm32 (no native-only deps).
- Each message carries a stable id for idempotent dedupe; op.result.request_id correlates to the op.request id.

title: Injectable clock for deterministic time
description:
## Outcome
An injectable time source (a Clock trait with now() returning unix seconds) used everywhere deadlines/timestamps are computed, so every time-based transition is deterministic under test.

## Why
The reconcile-loop state machine, invoice expiry, paid_through/soft_date, and downtime credit all key on wall-clock time. Without an injectable clock none of those paths are unit-testable.

## Scope
A Clock trait + a real SystemClock + a TestClock (settable/advanceable). Wire store and reconcile to take a Clock.

## Non-goals
Monotonic-vs-wall subtleties beyond the NTP assumption (a §16 minor risk).

## Dependencies
None (foundational root). Unblocks: store (lnrent-7fp.1), reconcile, and every time-based test.

## Verification
Unit: TestClock advance drives a fake deadline transition without sleeping.

## Spec refs
SPEC §6.5; eng-review T11.

## Acceptance Criteria
- TestClock can set and advance time; a reconcile deadline fires under TestClock with no real sleep.
- SystemClock returns wall-clock unix seconds.
- store and reconcile take a Clock by injection (no direct std time calls in those paths).

title: Buyer management-op dispatch (op.request -> recipe hook -> op.result)
description:
## Outcome
Operator-side dispatch of buyer-facing management operations (SPEC.md §7.4, ADR-0013). The daemon receives an `op.request` NIP-17 DM `{subscription_id, op, params}`, authorizes it, runs the recipe's declared `request`-kind operation hook, and returns `op.result {status, data|error}`. This is the operator half of the unified buyer `ops` interface; the buyer half is buyer-core (lnrent-7fp.13).

## Why
A buyer must manage the service they rented (WireGuard get-config, Fedimint admin-url/dkg, Hermes config/exec, and stop/restart/status everywhere) through ONE interface, without a per-service client and without arbitrary host shell. Recipes declare a narrow management surface; the daemon dispatches it deterministically. AI-free: every op runs a recipe hook, never an LLM (ADR-0013, §4.1).

## Scope
- Inbound `op.request` handling on top of the Nostr engine's DM primitives (lnrent-7fp.5), decoded via the wire codec (lnrent-7fp.19).
- **Authorization**: load the subscription via the store actor (lnrent-7fp.1); accept only if the DM sender pubkey == the subscription's `buyer_pubkey`, the op is declared by the subscription's recipe, and the subscription state permits it (M1a: ACTIVE only; suspended/terminated refuse — a per-op "available-when-suspended" manifest flag is a post-M1a refinement, not built here).
- **Durable idempotency (CRITICAL, codex)**: before running, upsert an `op_invocation` row keyed unique on `(sender_pubkey, request_id)` (the op.request `id`) via the store actor (lnrent-7fp.1). A declared op may be NON-idempotent (e.g. `restart`), so a duplicate `op.request` — NIP-17 redelivery, buyer retry after a dropped `op.result`, or daemon restart mid-op — MUST resend the cached (DONE/ERROR) or in-flight (RUNNING) result and MUST NOT re-run the hook. `op.result.request_id` correlates the reply.
- **Dispatch (request-kind only)**: resolve `Recipe::operation(op)`, run its `ops/<hook>` via the recipe runner (lnrent-7fp.6) with the lifecycle-hook I/O contract (JSON stdin: subscription + instance + validated op params + host facts; JSON stdout = `op.result.data`), run OFF the reconcile loop. The `hook` is a bare name resolved to `ops/<hook>`; reject any non-bare hook (traversal) — `Operation::hook_is_safe` / `Recipe::validate` (lnrent-7fp.6) is the gate.
- **Structured errors**: on failure return `op.result {status:"error", error:{code, message, retryable}}` with no `data`. `code` ∈ `unauthorized | unknown_op | invalid_params | not_active | timeout | hook_failed`; cap/redact hook stderr; an `unauthorized` reply must NOT reveal whether the `subscription_id` exists (someone else's sub and a nonexistent sub are indistinguishable to a non-buyer).
- Emit `op.result` back over NIP-17; the `op_invocation` row IS the durable audit record (op, subscription, sender, status, cached result/error).
- M1a proves this with the trivial recipe's minimal op(s) (`status` idempotent, `restart` non-idempotent — exercises the dedupe path).

## Non-goals
`interactive`-kind ops (shell / console / `logs -f` / file copy over the Iroh Native-connect session, §9.2) — those arrive with reachability (M1b+). Rich per-service ops (WireGuard get-config, Fedimint dkg, Hermes exec) ship with their recipes (M1b/M2). The buyer-side ops surface is lnrent-7fp.13.

## Constraints / failure handling
Op-param validation mirrors order-param validation (reject unknown/missing). A hook timeout/nonzero exit -> `op.result {status:error}`, never a daemon wedge (reuse the recipe runner's timeout + output-cap). Unauthorized or unknown-op requests get `op.result {status:error}` WITHOUT running any hook, and are logged. Duplicate `op.request` (same message id) is de-duped by the Nostr engine; ops are NOT assumed idempotent, so a deduped duplicate must not double-run the hook.

## Dependencies
Depends on: wire codec (lnrent-7fp.19, for op.request `id` / op.result `request_id`), Nostr engine (lnrent-7fp.5, inbound DM + reply primitives), recipe runner (lnrent-7fp.6, runs the op hook + validates operation declarations incl. hook-name safety), store actor (lnrent-7fp.1, `op_invocation` idempotency + subscription lookup for auth). Unblocks: the e2e op proof in lnrent-7fp.15.

## Verification
Integration over a local relay + the trivial recipe: an authorized `op.request` runs the hook and returns `op.result`; a DUPLICATE op.request (same sender+id) resends the cached result and the hook runs exactly once (proven with non-idempotent `restart`); an `op.request` from a non-buyer pubkey is refused without running the hook and without leaking subscription existence; an undeclared op name is refused; a hook that times out / exits nonzero returns a structured `op.result` error.

## Spec refs
SPEC §5.1 (op.request/op.result, durable idempotency), §7.4, §11 (op_invocation); ADR-0013; ADR-0001 (daemon sole writer), ADR-0002 (recipes trusted code).

## Acceptance Criteria
- An authorized `op.request {id, subscription_id, op:"status", params:{}}` runs the trivial recipe's `ops/status` and returns `op.result {request_id, status:"ok", data:...}`.
- A duplicate `op.request` (same sender_pubkey + `id`) returns the SAME cached `op.result` and the hook runs exactly once — verified with non-idempotent `restart`, across a simulated daemon restart mid-op.
- An `op.request` whose DM sender != the subscription's `buyer_pubkey` is refused with `op.result {status:"error", error.code:"unauthorized"}`, runs NO hook, and does not reveal whether the subscription exists.
- An `op.request` naming an op the recipe does not declare is refused (`unknown_op`, no hook run); a hook with a non-bare (traversal) path is rejected by validation, never executed.
- A management hook that exceeds its timeout or exits nonzero yields a structured `op.result` error (`timeout` / `hook_failed`) and does not wedge the reconcile loop.
- Every invocation is recorded durably in `op_invocation` (op, subscription, sender pubkey, state, cached result/error).
- M1a scope: only `request`-kind ops, ACTIVE subscriptions only; interactive/Iroh ops and the suspended-op `allowed_states` flag are explicitly out of scope here.

title: sqlite migrations (user_version)
description:
## Outcome
A minimal schema-version/migration mechanism (sqlite user_version) so the schema can evolve past M0 without a painful first column change.

## Why
store.rs currently applies CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS with no versioning; the first real migration would be ad hoc. Codex flagged this.

## Scope
A migrations runner keyed on PRAGMA user_version; M1a is version 1 (the current §11 schema).

## Non-goals
Down-migrations.

## Dependencies
Depends on: store (lnrent-7fp.1). Unblocks: any later schema change.

## Verification
Unit: opening an old (v0) db migrates to v1; opening a current db is a no-op.

## Spec refs
SPEC §11; eng-review T12; codex finding.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Opening a fresh db sets user_version = 1 and applies the §11 schema.
- Opening a current (v1) db is a no-op.
- Unit test: a simulated v0 db migrates to v1 without data loss.

title: Fedimint PaymentBackend (ecash via existing federation + gateway)
description:
## Outcome
A Fedimint-backed PaymentBackend — the PRIMARY receive path (ADR-0012). The operator's fedimint client (member of an EXISTING federation, routing through an EXISTING gatewayd) issues a gateway bolt11 bound to an external_id, receives into ecash, and exposes the §6.1 trait: create_invoice(amount, memo, expiry, external_id), lookup, pay (ecash-out refund via the gateway, returns a payment id), payment_status, and watch() — a settlement stream emitting ecash-received for an invoice's external_id.

## Why
Fedimint ecash is the economic fit for low-value rentals: NO per-payment inbound-liquidity cost (the gateway eats the LN side), unlike phoenixd whose first payment triggers a costly channel open (ADR-0012). phoenixd + LND are later secondary backends (M3). Fedimint cannot hold invoices either, so capture-then-refund applies (ADR-0003).

## Scope
fedimint client integration (existing federation + gateway); gateway-issued invoice carrying external_id; ecash-received settlement stream -> Settlement{invoice_id, external_id, amount_sat}; ecash-out refund via pay(); satisfies the §6.1 PaymentBackend trait stubbed in backends.rs (add FedimintPayment as the primary; the M0 PhoenixdPayment stub moves to M3).

## Non-goals
phoenixd + LND backends (M3); running a guardian (the operator JOINS an existing federation, ADR ref §9 fedimint recipe is separate).

## Constraints / failure handling
settlement = ecash received for the requested invoice; the client tracks which invoice -> which order (external_id, UNIQUE); refunds pay out via the gateway to the buyer's BOLT12 offer / LN address; gateway/federation unavailability surfaces as a backend error; settlements can be redelivered on client reconnect (capture stays idempotent).

## Dependencies
None (parallel root, lane B). Unblocks: capture (lnrent-7fp.8), refund (lnrent-7fp.11).

## Verification
Integration against a test federation + gateway (or a mock PaymentBackend for the e2e).

## Spec refs
SPEC §6.1, §6.4; ADR-0003, ADR-0009, ADR-0012.

## Acceptance Criteria
- create_invoice(.., external_id) returns a gateway bolt11; on payment, watch() emits Settlement{invoice_id, external_id, amount_sat} when ecash is received.
- A redelivered settlement (client reconnect) is replayable so capture stays idempotent.
- pay() pays a refund out via the gateway and returns a payment id; payment_status(id) reflects sent/failed.
- The §6.1 PaymentBackend trait is satisfied by the Fedimint impl; the e2e (lnrent-7fp.15) runs it against a mock.

title: Nostr engine: NIP-99 listings + NIP-17 DM protocol
description:
## Outcome
A Nostr engine that (a) publishes NIP-99 classified listings (kind 30402, with an operator tag AND the recipe's declared management operations) for the operator's offers, and (b) speaks the lnrent DM protocol over NIP-17 gift-wrapped DMs: order.request, order.invoice, order.error, provision.ready, billing.invoice, billing.notice, billing.refund, renew.request, sub.cancel. (The op.request/op.result DISPATCH is the operator op-dispatch bead lnrent-7fp.20, built on this engine's inbound-DM + reply primitives.)

## Why
The marketplace is Nostr-native (NIP-99 discovery + NIP-17 transaction); NIP-90 was rejected as ill-fitting. NIP-17 over public relays has no ordering/ack/delivery guarantee, so the protocol needs message IDs, idempotency, and buyer resync (codex/outside-voice P1).

## Scope
rust-nostr (nostr-sdk) integration; publish/replace 30402 listings (carrying the operator tag + the recipe's `[[operation]]` declarations, so buyers can discover the ops surface before ordering, §7.4); gift-wrap (NIP-59/44) encode/decode of the DM JSON; per-message id + idempotent handling of duplicates; publish/subscribe across several default relays; account-0 key signing (single key in M1a).

## Non-goals
Operator manifest / per-box key verification (M5); the web buyer shell (lnrent-7fp.18, also M1a). The durable outbox SENDER lives in the provision+outbox bead (lnrent-7fp.10); this bead provides the publish/subscribe primitives it uses. The op.request -> op.result DISPATCH (auth + run hook + reply) is lnrent-7fp.20; this bead only provides the inbound-DM + reply primitives it consumes.

## Constraints / failure handling
Relays drop events: publish to multiple relays, dedupe inbound by message id, support buyer resync (renew.request prompts redelivery). Do not trust gift-wrap created_at for expiry (use payload fields).

## Dependencies
Depends on: the wire codec (lnrent-7fp.19) — built ON it, does not own it. Unblocks: provision+outbox (lnrent-7fp.10), operator op-dispatch (lnrent-7fp.20). (The CLI buyer lnrent-7fp.13 depends on the codec lnrent-7fp.19, NOT on this operator engine.)

## Verification
Integration over a local relay.

## Spec refs
SPEC §5.1, §5.2, §5.3, §7.4; eng-review T5; codex.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A NIP-99 (30402) listing publishes and is fetchable from a local relay, carrying the operator tag AND the recipe's declared management operations.
- order.request -> order.invoice -> provision.ready round-trips over NIP-17 on a local relay.
- A duplicate inbound DM (same message id) is processed exactly once.
- A dropped order.invoice is recovered by the buyer re-sending order.request (idempotent by order/request id); renew.request covers only renewal resync.

## Note (codex)
This bead owns the NIP-17/NIP-99 TRANSPORT primitives (gift-wrap encode/decode, publish/subscribe across relays, message-id dedupe). The order/invoice business orchestration is owned by lnrent-7fp.17; the op.request/op.result management dispatch is owned by lnrent-7fp.20.

## Boundary (polish)
The wire codec (DM message types + NIP-17 gift-wrap + 30402 schema) is the shared lib lnrent-7fp.19. This bead is the OPERATOR-side engine — publish listings, handle inbound order + op DMs (decode/dedupe at the transport level, then hand order DMs to lnrent-7fp.17 and op DMs to lnrent-7fp.20), the outbox publish primitive, multi-relay publish/subscribe — built ON that codec with an injected signer (operator key from lnrent-7fp.16). It no longer owns the codec.

title: Recipe runner + trivial M1a recipe
description:
## Outcome
A hook runner that executes a recipe's lifecycle hooks (provision/suspend/resume/destroy/healthcheck) AND its buyer-facing management-operation hooks (`ops/<hook>`, §7.4) as child processes with: a timeout (kill on expiry), bounded stdout/stderr capture, JSON on stdin (subscription+instance+validated params+host facts), JSON result on stdout, run OFF the reconcile loop. Plus ONE trivial instant recipe for M1a (a WireGuard peer or a dummy 'echo creds' service) that also declares a minimal request-kind management op (`status` / `restart`) so the handshake AND the ops interface have something to exercise.

## Why
A hung/runaway provision must not wedge the reconcile loop (codex/eng-review). Recipes are trusted code (ADR-0002) but still fallible. M1a needs a fast, reliable recipe to prove the loop without VM complexity. Management ops (ADR-0013) run on the same hook-runner machinery, so the runner owns both.

## Scope
process spawn with timeout + output cap + JSON stdin/stdout (shared by lifecycle hooks and management-op hooks); Recipe::validate() (hooks exist+executable, backend known, os supported, AND `[[operation]]` declarations valid — kind in {request, interactive}; `hook` is a SAFE BARE NAME (no path separators, no `..`, no absolute) resolved as `ops/<hook>` — `Operation::hook_is_safe` (already in recipe.rs); and for request-kind the resolved `ops/<hook>` exists+executable, op names unique); the trivial recipe dir + lifecycle hooks + a minimal `ops/` hook.

## Non-goals
Incus/VM recipe (M1b); third-party recipe install (never, ADR-0002). The operator-side op DISPATCH (inbound op.request -> auth -> run hook -> op.result) is lnrent-7fp.20; this bead only provides the runner that executes an op hook and the validation of operation declarations.

## Constraints / failure handling
timeout -> kill + treat as provision/op failure -> refund path (provision) or op.result error (op); secrets via stdin JSON, not argv/env; idempotent lifecycle hooks (re-run safe) for crash recovery (management-op hooks are not assumed idempotent — that is handled at dispatch, lnrent-7fp.20).

## Dependencies
None (parallel root, lane E). Unblocks: provision+outbox (lnrent-7fp.10), operator op dispatch (lnrent-7fp.20).

## Verification
Unit + a smoke provision + a smoke management-op run.

## Spec refs
SPEC §7, §7.2, §7.4, §8.1; ADR-0002, ADR-0013; eng-review T8, T10.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A hook exceeding its timeout is killed and surfaces as failure (unit), for both lifecycle and management-op hooks.
- Oversized stdout/stderr is truncated to the cap, not unbounded.
- Nonzero exit surfaces as failure; Recipe::validate() rejects a recipe with a missing/non-executable hook, unknown backend, or unsupported OS.
- Recipe::validate() rejects an `[[operation]]` with an unknown `kind`, a non-bare/traversal `hook` (e.g. `../x`, `a/b`, absolute), a duplicate op name, or (for request-kind) a missing/non-executable `ops/<hook>`.
- The trivial recipe provisions and returns a delivery-payload JSON on stdout, AND its minimal request-kind op hook runs and returns JSON on stdout.

## Tier validation (codex)
- Recipe::validate() rejects a missing or invalid security tier (one of 0/1/1.5/2); the Listing carries an honest tier (ADR-0007, §9.1).

title: Pre-flight + capacity reservation
description:
## Outcome
Order-time capacity reservation plus order-param validation. At pre-flight (before the invoice is issued) the daemon validates the order's params against the recipe [[params]], checks available >= requested, and atomically (via the store actor) creates a reservation HELD for the order with TTL = invoice expiry. available = host budget - (active Instances + live reservations). Reservation released on expiry/refund, stays HELD through PROVISIONING, becomes CONSUMED at ACTIVE.

## Why
Pre-flight without reservation lets two concurrent orders race the last slot into capture-then-refund — on a one-box host the scarce resource (RAM/disk/ports) is the second sale, not an edge case (outside-voice P1). Params must be validated before money moves.

## Scope
operator-configured host budget; param validation against the recipe manifest; the reservation table lifecycle (HELD/CONSUMED/RELEASED); pre-flight check + reserve in one store transaction.

## Non-goals
Runtime per-Instance cgroup limits (the compute backend enforces those in M1b).

## Constraints / failure handling
reserve is atomic with the availability check (no TOCTOU); reservation stays HELD across the whole PROVISIONING window so a concurrent order can't reuse the slot mid-provision.

## Dependencies
Depends on: store (lnrent-7fp.1). Unblocks: e2e.

## Verification
Unit (concurrent reservation) + integration.

## Spec refs
SPEC §6.4, §9.3, §7.1; eng-review T9; outside-voice/codex.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Two concurrent orders for the last slot: exactly one reservation is HELD, the other rejected before any invoice (no TOCTOU).
- Order params are validated against the recipe [[params]] at pre-flight; invalid params reject before invoice.
- Reservation is RELEASED on invoice expiry, stays HELD through PROVISIONING, and becomes CONSUMED at ACTIVE.

title: Idempotent settlement capture
description:
## Outcome
Idempotent handling of ALL incoming settlements. On a Settlement, look up the invoice by external_id (UNIQUE), then UPDATE invoice SET status='PAID', settled_at=? WHERE id=? AND status='OPEN' in one transaction with the resulting subscription move:
- first invoice (sub PENDING) -> PROVISIONING (the capture);
- renewal invoice (sub ACTIVE) -> extend paid_through by period (early renewals stack);
- renewal while SUSPENDED (within retention) -> resume + extend paid_through.
A replayed settlement (backend redelivery) affects 0 rows and is a no-op, so paid_through can never double-extend.

## Why
the payment backend can redeliver a settlement (e.g. a Fedimint client / phoenixd ws reconnect); the event_log alone prevents double-running a hook, not double-counting a payment. The idempotency key is the invoice status guard, not the transition (codex #3/#5). Renewal and first payment share the same settlement machinery.

## Scope
settlement -> invoice (external_id) -> subscription mapping; the atomic status-guard transaction for first capture, renewal extension, and resume-on-late-renewal.

## Non-goals
Running provision (lnrent-7fp.10); refund execution (lnrent-7fp.11); deadline firing (lnrent-7fp.9 reconcile).

## Constraints / failure handling
external_id UNIQUE per invoice; one transaction per settlement; settlement for an already-PAID invoice is a no-op; settlement for an EXPIRED invoice routes to refund (settled-but-expired), not provisioning.

## Dependencies
Depends on: store (lnrent-7fp.1), phoenixd (lnrent-7fp.4). Unblocks: provision+outbox, refund, e2e.

## Verification
Unit + integration with duplicate and renewal settlements.

## Spec refs
SPEC §6.2, §6.3, §6.6; ADR-0009; eng-review T3; codex.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A duplicate Settlement for the same invoice extends paid_through exactly once (idempotent; unit + integration).
- A first-invoice settlement moves PENDING -> PROVISIONING; a renewal settlement extends paid_through; a renewal while SUSPENDED (in retention) resumes + extends.
- A Settlement for an EXPIRED invoice produces a refund_attempt, never a provision.
- UNIQUE(external_id) holds: a second insert with the same external_id fails.

## Settled-but-expired ownership (codex)
This bead only DETECTS a settlement on an EXPIRED invoice and ROUTES it: it creates sub REFUND_DUE + a refund_attempt (PENDING). The refund EXECUTION (pay/retry/status/alert) is owned by lnrent-7fp.11.

title: Reconcile loop + state machine + downtime credit
description:
## Outcome
A single periodic reconcile loop that advances subscriptions: it scans rows whose next_deadline <= now and fires the due transition (remind at soft_date, suspend at paid_through, destroy at retention end), recomputing next_deadline. Implements the full SubState machine (PENDING/PROVISIONING/ACTIVE/SUSPENDED/TERMINATED/EXPIRED/CANCELLED/REFUND_DUE/REFUNDED). Persists a heartbeat and, on restart, CREDITS operator downtime: any renewal/suspend deadline that fell in the downtime window is shifted by the outage and its reminder re-sent.
## Why
The daemon's core job is enforcing the prepaid-expiry lifecycle over time (ADR-0005). Transitions must be idempotent + journaled so a crash mid-hook can't double-run or wedge. Downtime credit (ADR-0005 revision) means a buyer is never suspended for the operator's outage (home-lab boxes reboot).
## Scope
the reconcile tick; all state transitions per §6.3; renewal extends paid_through (early stacks); heartbeat persist + downtime-credit deadline shift; next_deadline cursor.
## Non-goals
The capture (own bead), provision/deliver (own bead), refund execution (own bead) — reconcile triggers them via the store/commands.
## Constraints / failure handling
absolute wall-clock deadlines; idempotent transitions journaled to event_log; downtime credit on restart; reminders best-effort (buyer can renew.request on demand).
## Dependencies
Depends on: store, clock. Unblocks: refund, e2e.
## Verification
Unit (TestClock): each transition fires at its deadline; downtime-credit shifts a suspend that fell in the outage window and is NOT applied otherwise; missed deadlines fire on restart.
## Spec refs
SPEC §6.2, §6.3, §6.5; ADR-0005 (+ revision); eng-review T6.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Each transition fires at its deadline under TestClock (soft_date reminder, suspend at paid_through, destroy at retention).
- Downtime credit: a suspend whose deadline fell in the recorded outage window is shifted by the outage length; a suspend OUTSIDE the window is NOT shifted.
- Deadlines missed while the daemon was down fire on restart.

## Execution and messaging (codex)
This bead owns the state transitions AND their side effects: it fires the suspend/destroy hooks via the recipe runner (lnrent-7fp.6) and sends billing.notice + triggers the soft_date renewal invoice via the order-intake issuance path (lnrent-7fp.17) by ENQUEUEING outbox rows (store writes; the durable sender in lnrent-7fp.10 delivers them, exercised by the e2e). It RELEASES the reservation on TERMINATED (lnrent-7fp.7).
